text: Binary search solves the classic 'find target in sorted array' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n) search, O(1) space. Use it when the input is sorted and you need to locate an element efficiently. Implement by maintaining left and right pointers, computing mid = left + (right - left) / 2, then narrowing the search space based on whether arr[mid] equals, is less than, or greater than the target.

Binary search on answer space solves 'find minimum/maximum value satisfying a condition' pattern. Time complexity: O(log(max-min) * f(n)) where f(n) is the cost of the feasibility check. Use when the answer lies in a monotonic range and you can write a predicate that returns true/false. Binary search on the value range rather than an array index.

Lower bound binary search solves the 'find first position where arr[i] >= target' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n). Use when you need the leftmost valid insertion point. Implement with: while left < right, mid = left + (right - left) / 2, if arr[mid] < target then left = mid + 1 else right = mid. Return left.

Upper bound binary search solves the 'find first position where arr[i] > target' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n). Use when you need the rightmost valid insertion point or count of a value. Implement with: while left < right, mid = left + (right - left) / 2, if arr[mid] <= target then left = mid + 1 else right = mid. Return left.

Binary search on rotated sorted array solves the pattern: find target in an array that was sorted then rotated at an unknown pivot. Time complexity: O(log n). Use when the sorted invariant is partially preserved. Determine which half is properly sorted by comparing arr[mid] with arr[left], then decide which half to search based on whether target falls within the sorted half.

Binary search for peak element solves the 'find any local maximum in unsorted array' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n). Use when any peak is acceptable and neighboring comparisons are valid. If arr[mid] < arr[mid+1], peak is in the right half (left = mid + 1), else peak is in the left half including mid (right = mid). Return left when left == right.

Binary search with duplicates (find first/last occurrence) solves the 'search in array with repeated values' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n). Use when you need exact boundary indices of a target range. For first occurrence, when arr[mid] == target set right = mid; for last occurrence set left = mid + 1 and track the answer separately.

Binary search on 2D matrix solves the 'find target in row-wise and column-wise sorted matrix' pattern. Time complexity: O(log(m*n)). Use when matrix rows are sorted and each row starts after the last element of the previous row. Treat the matrix as a flattened sorted array: mid_val = matrix[mid / cols][mid % cols].

Binary search for minimum in rotated array solves the 'find the rotation pivot' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n). Use to locate the smallest element after an unknown rotation. If arr[mid] > arr[right], the minimum is in the right half (left = mid + 1), otherwise it is in the left half including mid (right = mid). Return arr[left].

Exponential search (doubling + binary search) solves the 'find target in unbounded or infinite sorted array' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n). Use when the array size is unknown. Double the index i until arr[i] >= target, then binary search within [i/2, i]. This avoids scanning from index 0 to an unknown bound linearly.

Binary search applied to 'Kth smallest in sorted matrix' pattern: find the K-th smallest value among all matrix values. Time complexity: O(n log(max-min)). Binary search on the value range; the feasibility check counts how many values are <= mid using a staircase traversal from the top-right corner in O(n) time.

Binary search for 'split array largest sum' (minimize the maximum subarray sum) solves the partition optimization pattern. Time complexity: O(n log(sum)). Use when you want to minimize a maximum or maximize a minimum across partitions. Binary search on the answer range [max_element, total_sum]; feasibility check verifies if k or fewer subarrays can be formed with each sum <= mid.

Binary search for square root / nth root estimation solves the 'find integer root without using math library' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n). Use when you need integer floor of a root. Binary search the range [1, n]; check mid*mid <= n (or mid^k <= n). The result is the largest mid where the condition holds.

Binary search on frequency: 'count of elements in range [lo, hi] in a sorted array'. Time complexity: O(log n). Use to answer range frequency queries efficiently. Apply upper_bound(hi) - lower_bound(lo) using two binary searches. Each call returns the boundary index, and their difference gives the count.

Binary search for 'median of two sorted arrays' solves the partition pattern. Time complexity: O(log(min(m,n))). Binary search on the smaller array to find a partition such that all elements on the left side are <= all elements on the right side across both arrays. Adjust partition using maxLeft1 <= minRight2 and maxLeft2 <= minRight1 conditions.

Ternary search solves the 'find maximum of a unimodal function' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n). Use when the function strictly increases then decreases (or vice versa). Divide range into thirds: if f(m1) < f(m2) search right third, else search left third. Not to be confused with binary search, but conceptually extends it to non-monotonic objectives.

Binary search for 'capacity to ship packages in D days' solves the minimum capacity optimization pattern. Time complexity: O(n log(sum)). Use for scheduling and resource allocation problems. Binary search on capacity in range [max_weight, total_weight]; feasibility check simulates the greedy loading and counts days needed.

Binary search with custom comparator solves 'find target in array of objects or transformed values' pattern. Time complexity: O(log n). Use when direct value comparison is replaced by a derived key or projection. Pass a key function and compare key(arr[mid]) against target_key instead of raw values. Works the same as standard binary search structurally.

Binary search off-by-one pitfalls: always use mid = left + (right - left) / 2 (not (left + right) / 2) to avoid integer overflow. For upper-bound variants, use mid = left + (right - left + 1) / 2 (ceiling division) to prevent infinite loops when left + 1 == right. The exit condition left == right ensures a single valid answer remains.

Binary search template summary: three standard templates. Template 1: while left <= right (find exact target, terminates when left > right). Template 2: while left < right with right = mid (find leftmost condition, terminates with left == right). Template 3: while left + 1 < right (keeps two-element window, terminates with exactly two candidates to check post-loop). Choose based on whether the invariant includes or excludes the boundary.
